coredata.dc:title: CQDs/biochar from reed straw modified Z-scheme MgIn2S4/BiOCl with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance for carbamazepine degradation in water
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Kemin
Song, Mengxi
Xie, Xiaoyun
Wen, Yuan
Wang, Zirun
Wei, Bin
Wang, Zhaowei
coredata.dc:description: The application of environmental-friendly and sustainable green materials in constructing photocatalysts to degrade pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) attracts more attention. Herein, biochar (BC) or biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used to modify MgIn2S4/BiOCl (MB) heterojunction photocatalyst with Z-scheme structure, and improved the photocatalytic degradation performance for carbamazepine (CBZ) in the aqueous solution. Both BC and CQDs could form electron transfer interface with MB heterojunction, resulting in the photodegradation rate of MgIn2S4/BiOCl/CQDs (MBC, 96.43%) and MgIn2S4/BiOCl/BC (MBB, 88.09%) to CBZ within 120 min visible-light irradiation, which were significantly higher than that of MB (65.84%). Moreover, photoelectrochemical and photoluminescence tests verified that CQDs could act as a bridge for storing and transferring electrons in the entire Z-scheme system. Thence, compared with MBB, MBC could produce more •OH and •O2 − under the visible light, which was indicated by the results of radical quenching experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance. Interestingly, under the natural sunlight, the photocatalytic performance of MBC to CBZ was even better than under laboratory conditions. In addition, the TOC removal efficiencies of MBB and MBC could reach 85.09% and 93.79% respectively, and ECOSAR program was utilized to further evaluate the eco-toxicity of CBZ and the intermediates towards fish, daphnid, and green algae, indicating that the photocatalytic process involving MBB and MBC showed outstanding toxicity reduction performance. Finally, compared with other composites, MBB and MBC showed higher photocatalytic performance and lower energy consumption, which would provide a green strategy for biochar materials in the photocatalytic treatment of PPCPs in water.

head.ce:title: CQDs/biochar from reed straw modified Z-scheme MgIn2S4/BiOCl with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performance for carbamazepine degradation in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of environmental-friendly and sustainable green materials in constructing photocatalysts to degrade pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) attracts more attention. Herein, biochar (BC) or biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were used to modify MgIn2S4/BiOCl (MB) heterojunction photocatalyst with Z-scheme structure, and improved the photocatalytic degradation performance for carbamazepine (CBZ) in the aqueous solution. Both BC and CQDs could form electron transfer interface with MB heterojunction, resulting in the photodegradation rate of MgIn2S4/BiOCl/CQDs (MBC, 96.43%) and MgIn2S4/BiOCl/BC (MBB, 88.09%) to CBZ within 120 min visible-light irradiation, which were significantly higher than that of MB (65.84%). Moreover, photoelectrochemical and photoluminescence tests verified that CQDs could act as a bridge for storing and transferring electrons in the entire Z-scheme system. Thence, compared with MBB, MBC could produce more •OH and •O2 − under the visible light, which was indicated by the results of radical quenching experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance. Interestingly, under the natural sunlight, the photocatalytic performance of MBC to CBZ was even better than under laboratory conditions. In addition, the TOC removal efficiencies of MBB and MBC could reach 85.09% and 93.79% respectively, and ECOSAR program was utilized to further evaluate the eco-toxicity of CBZ and the intermediates towards fish, daphnid, and green algae, indicating that the photocatalytic process involving MBB and MBC showed outstanding toxicity reduction performance. Finally, compared with other composites, MBB and MBC showed higher photocatalytic performance and lower energy consumption, which would provide a green strategy for biochar materials in the photocatalytic treatment of PPCPs in water.

ce:section.ce:para: As pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) are excessively consumed on a global scale, their existence and their degradation products in the aquatic environment have potential effects on humans and wildlife (Heberer T., 2002; Focazio et al., 2008). Carbamazepine (CBZ), one of the most extensively used pharmaceuticals in the field of treating epilepsy, trigeminal neuralgia, and other mental diseases, has been identified as a future emerging priority candidate in 2007 (Pruden A., 2014; Clara et al., 2004). Furthermore, because of the existence of benzene rings and π bonds in the structure of CBZ, the removal efficiency of CBZ in sewage is relatively low through traditional methods (Ioanna et al., 2013). In this case, photocatalytic technology has shown great potential in natural water and wastewater treatment due to its low cost, environment-friendly, and sustainability (Bahmani et al., 2020; Amir et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2009).

Semiconductor photocatalysis technology can generate photo-generated carriers and active oxygen groups with strong redox ability by irradiation, thereby degrading and converting many pollutants like PPCPs and heavy metals (Fularz et al., 2020). Bismuth-based photocatalysts have received widespread attention due to their chemical stability, low toxicity, low cost, and high photocatalytic efficiency (Kato et al., 2017; Mohan, R., 2010; Casagrande et al., 2020). BiOCl shows outstanding photocatalytic properties among these photocatalysts because of the layered structure with [Bi2O2]2+ slabs (Cheng et al., 2014; Ye et al., 2014; Mafa et al., 2021). BiOCl prepared by different methods have different morphologies, such as flower-ball-like and nanosheets. They all have a unique effect on dye photosensitization and pollutant degradation (Liu et al., 2020b; Zhao et al., 2020). However, due to the low solar-light energy utilization efficiency and fast recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, the monomer photocatalyst is relatively limited in practical applications. Constructing a heterojunction system is one of the pivotal ways to improve the performance of monomer photocatalysts, such as type Ⅱ system and Z-scheme system, which can promote the photo-generated electron-hole pair separation efficiency by change the electronic structure and crystal structure of the composite materials, thereby broadening the corresponding range of spectral. Significantly, the Z-scheme system stands out from different types of heterojunction, which can maintain a high redox potential. It can form photo-generated carriers with strong reduction-oxidation properties to efficiently degrade organic pollutants and split water overall under visible light (Kristy et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2014; Dibyananda et al., 2020; Boon-Junn et al., 2021; Malefane et al., 2020).

Considering that the band gap of BiOCl is around 3.2 eV and it is difficult to respond to visible light (Tian et al., 2019). Therefore, constructing a heterojunction is an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance of BiOCl. In recent years, ternary metal sulfides such as ZnIn2S4, CuIn2S4, AgInS2 have attracted more attention due to narrow bandgap and efficient absorption capacity (Liu et al., 2020a; Deng et al., 2019; Patrycja et al., 2020). MgIn2S4, with an inverse spinel structure, has a narrow bandgap around 2.1–2.28 eV (Prasad et al., 2001; Guo et al., 2019). BiOCl as a representative of BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) with wide bandgap energy around 3.2 eV, has been considered to be a promising photocatalyst to form Z-scheme heterojunction (Mafa et al., 2020). Thence, combining BiOCl and MgIn2S4 (MgIn2S4, which has a cross-band gap structure compared with BiOCl) is expected to form a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, thereby enabling the heterojunction with advanced photocatalytic through decreasing the charge recombination and improving the charge transferability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of nano-carbon material with conjugated structure and up-conversion photoluminescence properties, have a size of about 10 nm (Si et al., 2020; Marta et al., 2021; Duo et al., 2016). Notably, CQDs possess excellent photoelectron transferability, brilliant conductivity, abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, so it has great potential in the field of PPCPs removal in water (Wang et al., 2019a; Yang et al., 2013; Leila et al., 2020; He et al., 2019). Also, because it can improve the visible light-harvesting performance of other monomer photocatalysts, CQDs will be promising photocatalyst and carbon materials (Anastasios et al., 2021). Biomass CQDs prepared from reed straws have the same advantages as CQDs made by traditional chemical methods, but the former is more environmentally friendly. Biochar (BC) is prepared by pyrolysis of agricultural wastes biomass such as cauliflower root and reed straw (Liang et al., 2020a; Zhang et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017a). BC has been widely applied in environmental fields such as sewage purification and PPCPs removal as a cheap and environmentally friendly material. Because of its large specific surface area and good electrical conductivity, BC can be used as an excellent platform to support photocatalytic nano-materials and decrease the recombination of photo-generated e−-h+ pairs. Therefore, the bio-mass CQDs and biochar modified MgIn2S4/BiOCl heterojunction photocatalysts with simulated visible light for PPCPs photodegradation will provide a new idea to design photocatalysts for the environmental remediation.

Herein, based on the perspective of environment-friendly and improving the carrier transmission rate of the heterojunction interface, biomass CQDs and BC from reed straw were successfully introduced into the Z-scheme MgIn2S4/BiOCl heterojunction system through a two-step method. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity of the composite photocatalysts for PPCPs degradation was evaluated by using CBZ as a model pollutant under visible-light irradiations. In the first place, green and novel photocatalysts MgIn2S4/BiOCl/BC (MBB) and MgIn2S4/BiOCl/CQDs (MBC) were fabricated successfully by hydrothermal and hydrolysis methods. Secondly, it focused on the differences in the morphology, light absorption capacity, and photocatalytic performance of MBB and MBC. Thirdly, in combination with practical applications, the photodegradation performance of CBZ under the LED visible light and natural sunlight and the regeneration and stability of the MBB and MBC were studied. Moreover, the main reactive oxygen species produced under LED visible light in the system were determined by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) and quenching experiments, and the mechanisms of introducing BC and CQDs to enhance the photocatalytic performance were proposed. Finally, this work evaluated the toxicity of CBZ and its photodegradation products during the photocatalysis system towards MBB and MBC by using ECOSAR program.
Detailed chemicals and reagents information was listed in Text S1 of Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of biochar (BC) and green carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The collected reed straw was washed with distilled water and dried at 80 °C. After that, the reed straw was crushed into slag to prepared BC and CQDs. The reed straw BC was synthesized via the pyrolysis method at lower oxygen levels in a muffle furnace with a temperature of 500 °C for 5 h (There were about 100 g reed straw biomass in each crucible) (Yuan et al., 2011). Then soaked and cleaned several times with distilled water and dried at 80 °C. BC was milled to pass a 100-mesh sieve and preserved in a glass bottle. The reed straw biomass CQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method. 20 mL distilled water and 0.5 g reed straw biomass after being crushed were mixed and stirred for 30 min, then transferred to 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 4 h. The prepared solution was collected to dialyzed (1000 Da) for 24 h and stored at 4 °C. The comparison image of biomass CQDs and CQDs synthesized by chemical reagents was shown in Figure S2. Compared with CQDs synthesized by chemical reagents, biomass CQDs are bright yellow solutions with a delicate smell of plants.

MIS was prepared by the one-step hydrothermal method as previously reported (Jing et al., 2019). 0.5 mmol MgCl2·6H2O and 1 mmol InCl4 were distributed into 70 mL ethylene glycol and then to ultrasound until the solution became transparent. Next, stirred for 30 min after adding 4 mmol TAA into the solution. After that, the solutions were transferred to 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. The prepared MIS was washed with distilled water and ethanol several times, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The BOC was prepared by a one-step hydrolysis method (Li et al., 2016). 2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was distributed into 20 mL ethylene glycol and ultrasound for 30 min, which was marked as A solution. Then 2 mmol NaCl was dissolved in 20 mL distilled water and marked as B solution. Mixed A and B solutions and stirred for 12 h at room temperature. Similarly, the prepared BOC was washed with distilled water and ethanol several times, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The MBB and MBC composites were prepared by the two-steps method. The pure MIS, BC, and CQDs were synthesized in the first step according to the above method. It should be noted that the ratios of MIS, BC and CQDS used to synthesize MBB and MBC were determined by the preliminary experiments (The detailed results and discussion were showed in Text S2). 0.1042 g MIS and 0.0260 g BC were dispersed in ethylene glycol respectively and mixed with A solution (2 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) by stirred, the B solution (2 mmol NaCl) was mixed slowly with A solution when the color of the solution changed from maroon to gray to prepare MBB. Similarly, replaced BC with CQDs (5 mL) to prepare MBC. MBB and MBC were washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times, then dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The synthesis of MB was basically the same as above, but the BC/CQDs were not added.

The structure and crystallinity of photocatalysts were ascertained through X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D2 PHASER, Bruker AXS, Germany). The surface morphologies of photocatalysts were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (JSM-6701 F, JFOL, Japan). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS) and element-mapping (IE250, Oxford Instruments, UK) were used to observe the microstructure of composites and analyze their constituent elements. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CQDs and MBC composites were examined by FEI TECNAI G2 (USA). The surface element compositions and valence state of the photocatalysts were detected by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). Surficial functional groups of BC, CQDs, MBB, and MBC were observed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nexus 670, Thermo-Nicolet, USA). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) analyzed the optical absorption property of the photocatalysts (Lambda 950, PerkinElmer, USA). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) parameters of products were provided via N2 adsorption-desorption surface area instrument (ASAP, 2010; USA). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments were detected by a Bruker A300–9.5/12 (Germany) spectrometer using 5.5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) to trap superoxide radicals (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of BOC, MB, MBB, and MBC were investigated through a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Excitation-dependent PL spectra of biomass CQDs were identified by a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Leaching of bismuth ions, magnesium ions, and indium ions in final reaction solution was evaluated through high resolution inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Plasma Quant PQ 9000, Analytik Jena, Germany).

The target pollutant of photodegradation was CBZ, which was used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts. The composite (50 mg) was put into CBZ solution (10 mg/L, 50 mL). After stirring for 30 min in the dark to keep the adsorption/desorption equilibrium, a 50 W LED lamp with 475 nm dominant wavelengths was used as a visible light source (Figure S3 showed the detailed wavelength ranges). 2 mL of the solution was taken out and then depurated with water system membrane filters (0.22 μm) for the after test.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response (PCR) were conducted by CS350H electrochemical workstation (Wuhan CorrTest Instruments Corp. Ltd.) to investigate the photocatalytic activity of photocatalysts. In this work, used Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode, platinum wires as a counter electrode, and indium-tin-oxide (ITO) glass which coating photocatalysts on as a working electrode. The working electrode was prepared as follows. 10 mg of the samples were dispersed in 1 mL ethylene glycol and then coated on indium-tin-oxide (ITO) sheet glass. 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution was used for EIS and PCR measurements. The light source was a 50 W LED lamp and all photoelectrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature.
Analytical methods of CBZ concentration and eco-toxicity of degradation products were illustrated in Text S3 of Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologic Structure surface analysis of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: SEM was used to characterize the surface morphology of the photocatalysts. It can be observed that pure BOC was presented nanosheets with a diameter of about 100 nm in Fig. 1 (a), which had an agglomeration phenomenon. For pure MIS, it exhibited a nanoflower-shaped structure with ca. 0.5 μm–2 μm diameter from Fig. 1(b). The morphologies of MB (Fig. 1(c)) showed that BOC nanosheets and MIS nanoflowers coexisted and BOC nanosheets were evenly distributed on the petals of MIS nanoflowers, respectively. However, the surface morphology of MBC was similar to MB (Fig. 1(e)), which might be because the size of CQDs was too small to be observed and the content was relatively low. Fig. 1(d) illustrated that BC had smooth surfaces and porous structure, which could provide a large number of loading sites for the photocatalyst. Meanwhile, it also could be clearly observed from Fig. 1(f) that the composite materials were dispersed and attached to the BC, and the surface of BC changed rougher. The EDS mapping and the element component ratio of MBB and MBC were illustrated in Fig. 1(g) and (h), which proved the successful introduction of CQDs and BC in MBC and MBB. In addition, the Bi, O, Cl, Mg, In, S, and C were all existed in photocatalysts and distributed evenly.

TEM could display the microstructures and morphologies of CQDs and MBC well. From the TEM image of CQDs (Fig. 1(i)), the small black dots were CQDs with a diameter between 5 and 10 nm. For MBC (Fig. 1(j and k)), it was clearly observed that the thinner nanosheets of MIS nano-flowers were covered by BOC nanosheets. In the view of HRTEM results of MBC in Fig. 1(l), the lattice distance of about 0.32 nm was corresponding to the (311) plane of MIS (Gayatri et al., 2020), and 0.75 nm was belonging to the (001) plane of BOC with layered structure (Wang et al., 2019b). Fortunately, the clear lattice fringes of 0.22 nm were referred to the (100) plane of CQDs, indicating that CQDs were successfully introduced into the MB heterojunction.

The specific surface area and pore-size distribution of different materials were compared by the BET specific surface area (SBET), total pore volume, external surface area, and average pore diameter distribution of different materials were shown in Table S1. SBET of MBB and MBC were increased slightly to 44.63 m2/g and 46.48 m2/g, respectively, compared with and MB (43.39 m2/g) after introducing biochar and CQDs. In addition, the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of composite photocatalysts as shown in Figure S4 could be manifested as type IV with H3 hysteresis loop, which illustrated the existence of mesoporous.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition, texture properties, and surface structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in Fig. 2 , the crystalline structures and crystallinity of photocatalysts were investigated by XRD measurement and the chemical states of these were studied through XPS analysis. In Fig. 2(a), the characteristic peaks of pure BOC and MIS were comfortably matched well with their monoclinic phases (pdf#: 06–0249 and pdf#: 31–0792). For bare BOC nanosheets, where the typical diffraction peaks at 11.9°, 24.1°, 32.5°, 33.4°, 49.7°, and 60.5° were individually assigned to diffraction planes of (001), (002), (110), (102), (113) and (114). Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks of the pure MIS nanoflowers were at 27.6°, 33.4°, and 48.0° corresponded well to the facet of (311), (400), and (440). The XRD patterns of MB, MBB, and MBC composites were basically the same as BOC, which explained the introduction of MIS, BC, and CQDS would not significantly affect the crystal structure of BOC. However, the diffraction peaks of BC and CQDs could not be found in the MBB and MBC composites, which might be due to the less content of BC and CQDs on the MBB and MBC composite. Then, the surface element components of BOC, MIS, MBB, and MBC were compared by XPS analysis. The survey wide spectra of them in Fig. 2(b) showed the signal of Bi, Cl, and O elements in BOC, Mg, In, and S elements in MIS, and Bi, Cl, O, Mg, In, S and C coexisted in MBB and MBC, which illustrated that BC and CQDs were doped in the composites, the successful preparation of the photocatalysts was more certain.

The FTIR spectrums of CQDs, MBB, BC, and MBB were displayed in Figure S5 (a and b), which could be clearly observed that BC and CQDs were rich in functional groups. In the spectrum of CQDs, the strong absorption peak at 1038 cm−1 and 1624 cm−1 could be identified as C–H stretching and CO functional groups, 2961 cm−1 and the range of 3012–3707 cm−1 illustrated the existence of C–H and -O-H groups (Thanigaivelan et al., 2020). Besides, the sharp band at 1518 cm−1 was assigned to the C–N vibration. The peak located at around 770 cm−1 confirmed the existence of CC (Thanigaivelan et al., 2020). As for MBC, it showed a sharp band at 1382 cm−1 which could be assigned to MIS (Chen et al., 2017b). Moreover, a shrill and robust absorption peak at 527 cm−1 belonged to Bi–O could be identified in the preparation of photocatalyst successfully (Tian et al., 2019). Similarly, BC also contained abundant chemical bonds and functional groups, the peaks at 1600 cm−1and 1082 cm−1 belonged to CO stretching and C–H stretching (Ding et al., 2016). The broad peak in the range of 3213–3653 cm−1 for BC was observed, which represented -O-H groups (Yang et al., 2019b).

The C 1s at 284.6 eV was employed to rectify binding energy analyses. The peaks at 159.49 and 164.86 eV were assigned to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, which were the characteristics of Bi3+ in BOC nanosheets, respectively (Fig. 2(c)). And XPS peaks of Bi3+ shifted slightly to higher binding energies in MBB and MBC, which might be attributed to the strong interface interaction between BOC and MIS. The O 1s peaks (Fig. 2(d)) of BOC were fitted into two peaks at 530.40 and 532.94 eV. The peak at 530.40 eV was characteristic of the Bi–O bonds in [Bi2O2]2+ of BOC lattice, while the peak at 532.94 eV was ascribed to oxygen vacancies (OVs) (Ding et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019). For MBB, in addition, to represent the binding energy of Bi–O at 530.29 eV, and a new characteristic peak appeared at 531.19 eV, which might be the peak of –OH introduced by the doping of BC (Fan et al., 2018). Similarly, due to the doping of CQDs, there were three XPS peaks in MBC, the O 1s was assigned to Bi–O (530.20 eV), O–H (530.7 eV), and OVs (532.22 eV) (He et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2017). For Fig. 2(e), the peaks at 198.49 and 199.91 eV were related to the Cl 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbited of Cl−. In MBB and MBC, the binding energies of Cl 2p only slightly changed. Fig. 2(f) showed the peak at 1303.51 eV could be corresponded to Mg 1s, indicating the existence of Mg2+. The large migration of Mg elements of MBB and MBC might be due to the reduction of electron cloud density and the weakening of electron binding ability (Jiang et al., 2020). In 3 d spectra (Fig. 2(g)) of MIS was distinguished by two symmetric peaks at 444.67 and 452.41 eV, corresponding to the In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2. The peaks of S 2p for MIS at 161.56 and 162.67 eV in Figure S6 were respectively ascribed to S2−. However, due to the high content of Bi element in MBB and MBC, and the binding energy of Bi 4f was close to that of S 2p, the peaks of S 2p in the MBB and MBC were not obvious, might be covered by the peak of Bi 4f (Guo et al., 2019). The signal peaks at 284.87 eV, 285.55 eV, and 289.16 eV in Fig. 2(h) were indicated the existence of CC, C–O, and CO of MBC, which meant that CQDs were attached to the surface of MB by specific physical and chemical adsorption. And the spectra of C 1s for MBB was similar to MBC, except the binding energies were lower than that of MBC (Wang et al., 2012). Prominently, the binding energies of Bi 4f for MBB and MBC were higher than that of pure BOC, while the binding energies of Mg 1s and In 3 d were lower than that of pure MIS. Thence, it is reasonable to infer that the photo-generated charges will be transferred between MIS and BOC through BC or CQDs. And the migration direction of photo-generated electrons was from the BOC to the MIS, which was beneficial to separate photo-generated e−-h+ and improved the photocatalytic performance (Zhou et al., 2021; Ding et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photoelectrochemical properties of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of pure BOC and MIS, compound MB, MBC, and MBB were shown in Fig. 3 (a). The pure MIS had a broad UV–visible absorption and its absorption tail was around 600 nm (Gayatri et al., 2020). The bandgap absorption edge of BOC was around 380 nm, suggesting that BOC was weakly driven under visible light. After combining the BOC and MIS, the optical absorption ability of MB in the visible light region was stronger. In comparison with them, a higher absorption intensity in the visible region was observed of MBB and MBC, which meant that introducing BC and CQDs would make the heterojunction easier to be excited under the visible light. Especially for MBB, the absorption intensity in the visible region was the strongest due to the absorption of black-colored biochar. Meanwhile, according to the Schuster-Kubelka-Munk function, the bandgap energies of all the photocatalysts were determined (Gayatri et al., 2020). Fig. 3(b) exhibited that the values of bandgap energies of photocatalysts were 3.25 eV, 1.96 eV, 2.90 eV, 1.41 eV, and 1.82 eV for bare BOC, bare MIS, MB, MBC, and MBB, respectively. Compared to pure BOC and MIS, adding CQDs and BC could make the composite photocatalysts have smaller bandgap energy, which had a beneficial effect on the photocatalytic performance for CBZ degradation. As we all know, a smaller bandgap could use less energy and time for electronic excitation, thereby improving the light excitation rate.

In addition to DRS, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response (PCR) were used to demonstrate the transferred property of charge carriers and the photocurrent response of composites. As shown in Fig. 3(c), compared with other photocatalysts, the Nyquist arc radii of MBC and MBB were smaller, which meant that they had lower electrochemical impedance under the visible light irradiation. Fig. 3(d) displayed the transient photocurrent response of pure MIS, BOC, MB, MBB, and MBC composites under visible light irradiation at an interval of 50 s. It was obviously observed that MBC had the highest photocurrent among all photocatalysts. The results indicated that MBB and MBC achieved lower photo-generated carrier transfer resistance and faster transfer of interfacial charges, which further demonstrated that the addition of carbon does improve the transfer ability of electrons, elucidating that both biochar and bio-mass CQDs could act as charge transport bridge of the MgIn2S4/BiOCl heterojunction system. However, for MBB, limited by the morphology and thickness of BC, its photocurrent was lower than that of MBC (Zhang et al., 2018; Ryan et al., 2018).

The excitation-dependent PL spectra of biomass CQDs with different excitation wavelengths from 310 to 450 nm showed in Figure S7. With the low wavelength excitation, the CQDs displayed a weak intensity and broad emission spectrum, which gradually became stronger and narrower with the increase of the excitation wavelengths until 360 nm. When the excitation wavelength was further increased, it would cause a decrease in the intensity of the emission peak and a redshift of the emission wavelength, which may be caused by the π-π* transition of aromatic hydrocarbons in CQDs (Abhilasha et al., 2020). Also, because of the up-conversion fluorescence effect of CQDs, MBC had the smallest bandgap energy (Li et al., 2010). The photoluminescence spectrum (PL) could better characterize the recombination rate of photo-generated charges. In Figure S8(a and b), the photocatalysts showed the strongest emission peak at 464 nm and 600 nm because of the fast recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Obviously, PL emission intensity of MBC and MBB were much lower than pure BOC, pure MIS, and binary composite, revealing that photo-generated carrier could be separated and transferred by doped with BC and CQDs (Chen et al., 2020; Di et al., 2016).

In order to demonstrate the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples, the degradation of CBZ under visible light was investigated. The results were shown in Fig. 4 (a). The photocatalytic degradation rate of pure BOC, MIS, and MB composites were 59.32%, 50.67%, and 65.84% in 120 min under the LED illumination. Surprisingly, when BC and CQDs were added to the composites, the degradation efficiency was greatly improved. The MBB had a better degradation rate of 88.09% and MBC had the best degradation rate of 96.43%. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ conformed to the pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig. 4(b)). The degradation kinetics equation is as follows:
(1)
− ln
C t
C 0
= k t
where C0 and Ct represent the initial and after irradiation concentration of CBZ,
k
is the kinetic constant. The
k
of BOC, MIS, MB, MBB and MBC were 0.0071 min−1, 0.0060 min−1, 0.0077 min−1, 0.0159 min−1, and 0.0256 min−1, respectively. Thus, the rate constant of MBB was 2.44 times of pure BOC and 2.98 times of pure MIS. Furthermore, MBC was 3.86 times higher than BOC and 4.71 times than MIS. Compared with the previously reported photocatalysts, the as-prepared MBB and MBC also showed outstanding CBZ photodegradation ability, and Table S2 listed the detailed comparison between as-prepared samples and other photocatalysts (Gao et al., 2018, 2019, 2020, 2021; Xie et al., 2021; Li et al., 2019). In other words, CQDs could increase the visible light absorption capacity and facilitate the charge separation, which would realize the better performance of photodegrading CBZ (Di et al., 2016). These results clearly inferred that the CQDs and BC loading were favorable for the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ.

Solar energy utilization and energy conservation have always been important issues in the environmental field. At the same time, in order to evaluate the photocatalytic ability of MBC and MBB in the actual environment, the degradation of CBZ under natural sunlight was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 (c and d), the degradation rates of CBZ by MBC and MBB at 120 min were 97.41% and 84.99%, both MBB and MBC presented excellent degradation efficiency of CBZ, especially MBC, which sufficiently proved the potential of the composites in practical applications.

The reusability and stability of MBB and MBC were investigated through the recycling experiments for CBZ degradation under visible light. As shown in Figure S9 (a and b), the MBB showed a more distinctly decrease of photocatalytic activity for CBZ degradation after consecutive reaction for four times, while the MBC composite photocatalyst only exhibited 7.22% attenuation of photocatalytic activity at the same reaction conditions. Meanwhile, the crystal structure of MBB and MBC after four-recycled were evaluated by XRD. Figure S9(c) showed that the intensity of MBC diffraction peaks was decreased slightly, but there was no significant change in the crystal structure compared with fresh MBC, which illustrated MBC had excellent reusability and stability. However, for MBB, it was not difficult to find that the diffraction peak corresponding to the (001) crystal plane of BOC had changed significantly, and all the characteristic peaks shifted towards the low angle. Although the results indicated that the intensity of MBB diffraction had not changed much, its cycle stability and reusability reduced. In addition, the leaching of bismuth, magnesium, and indium ions in the final reaction solution was evaluated through the high resolution inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES). The dissolution rates of metal ions (bismuth, magnesium, and indium ions) after the photodegradation for CBZ of MBB and MBC were exhibited in Table S3, which illustrated the environmental friendliness of the obtained photocatalysts. Moreover, the ability of photo-generated reactive oxygen species is related to the surface oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of BC, especially surface carboxyl groups (Chen et al., 2017a). The FTIR spectrum (Figure S5) showed that the functional groups of C–O, C–H, and -O-H still existed in MBB and MBC after the reaction, indicating that they had certain stability. However, the characteristic peak of CO in MBB disappeared and the contents of C–O decreased from 58.2% to 40.9% after CBZ photodegradation Figure S9(d (Ⅱ)), indicating that CO, C–O participated in the photocatalytic reaction for CBZ photodegradation (Meng et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2017a; Xie et al., 2019). This might also be one reason why the photocatalytic activity of MBB decreased in subsequent repeated experiments. Finally, it could be clearly seen that the change of C–O, CO, and CC in the C 1s XPS spectra of MBC, was not obvious in Figure S9(d (Ⅰ)), which might be due to the stability of CQDs. The above results further verified that the MBC had good structural stability.

To distinguish the predominant free radicals of the MBB and MBC system about the photocatalytic degradation of CBZ, radical quenching experiments were evaluated by using different scavengers. BQ, IPA, and EDTA-2Na with a concentration of 0.5 mM were used as the scavengers of superoxide radicals (•O2 −), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and holes (h+). As shown in Fig. 5 (a and b), with the addition of BQ, IPA, and EDTA-2Na, the photo-degradation efficiency of CBZ were reduced by 70.2%, 11.8%, and 27.2% in MBB, while 77.6%, 21.1%, and 17.3% in MBC. Importantly, when BQ was mixed in the reaction, the degradation of CBZ by the two composites was inhibited significantly, suggesting that •O2 − was the dominating active radicals for both MBB and MBC systems. In addition, h+ and •OH also had an inhibitory effect on the photodegradation of CBZ by MBB and MBC. In Fig. 5(c and d), EPR tests exhibited that both MBB and MBC had signals of •OH and •O2 − under visible light irradiation. However, the signal intensity of generated •OH and •O2 − in MBC were higher than MBB. The addition of BQ and IPA had a more significant impact on MBC degradation system, indicating that MBC could generate more free radicals under visible light excitation for CBZ degradation.

Based on the characterization and experimental results, the mechanism of MBC and MBB photocatalytic degradation of CBZ under visible light could be explained (Scheme 1 ). First of all, according to the previous research, it was easy to know that the VB of MIS and BOC were 1.26 V vs. NHE and 3.55 V vs. NHE, respectively (Jing et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). And from the results of DRS, the bandgap of pure MIS could be determined as 1.96 eV, then inferred that the valence band (VB) and the conduction band (CB) of pure MIS was 1.26 V vs. NHE and −0.70 V vs. NHE, respectively. In the same way, the VB of pure BOC was 3.55 V vs. NHE and CB was 0.3 V vs. NHE could be inferred. However, based on the results of radical scavenger and EPR experiments, the conventional type-II heterojunction mechanism could not explain the process of CBZ degradation well because the CB potential of BOC was more positive than •O2 − (Eө (O2/•O2 −) = −0.33 V vs NHE), while the VB potential of MIS was more negative than •OH (Eө (H2O/•OH) = 2.4 V vs NHE), they would not product •O2 − and •OH in the MIS/BOC heterojunction system (Scheme S1). In detail, under the excitation of light, the photo-generated e− in the CB of BOC could be transferred and recombined with h+ in the VB of MIS by passing through CQDs or BC. The recombination of photo-generated carriers would cause the photo-generated e− in MIS to remain in the CB of MIS and the h+ in BOC to remain in the VB of BOC. Importantly, The CB of MIS was more negative than Eө (O2/•O2 − = −0.33 V vs NHE), and the VB of BOC was more positive than the Eө (H2O/•OH = 2.4 V vs NHE). This meant that the e− on CB of MIS could form •O2 − by trapped O2, whereas the h+ in the VB of BOC have enough oxidation ability to oxidize H2O to produce •OH, which matched with the EPR results. Consequently, according to the MIS/BOC heterojunction, Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism was more suitable and convincing. Compared with type II heterojunction system, electrons and holes with stronger redox ability would be left on the CB of MIS and the VB of BOC in the Z-scheme heterojunction system.

Besides, the high-energy or low-energy shifts of binding energy in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f and Mg 1s could determine a heterojunction with electron transfer mechanism. Generally, the high-energy or low-energy shifts of binding energy indicated a decrease or increase in electron density. From the results of XPS (Fig. 2), the Bi 4f state of MBC and MBB shifted to the high energy region compared with pure BOC. Meanwhile, the Mg 1s state of MBC and MBB shifted to the low energy region compared with pure MIS. The results indicated that an internal built-in electric field from BOC to MIS was formed, which benefited the separation and transfer of photo-generated carries via the Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism (Zhou et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020).

The addition of BC and CQDs could make the visible light more fully utilized. The formed MBB and MBC would better enhance the light absorption and photocatalytic activity of the MB heterojunction, which were verified by the spectra of UV–vis DRS. When the BC was added, the electrons of BOC would transfer from BC to MIS, thereby achieving better photocatalytic performance. In addition, in terms of morphology (from Fig. 1), MB composites were loaded on BC while the CQDs were loaded on MB composites. Due to the larger size of BC, the electron transfer path between MIS and BOC was longer in MBB than that in MBC, resulting in a slower speed of electron transfer, which was an important reason why the photocatalytic performance of MBB was lower than that of MBC (Ryan et al., 2018). Besides, due to the good conductivity and electron storage function of CQDs, the vector transfer of electrons from BOC to MIS can be realized, which was also consistent with the analysis results of XPS (Bi et al., 2021). And because of the up-conversion fluorescence performance of CQDs, long-wavelength light could be converted into short-wavelength light, more electron-hole pairs were excited, which would promote more free radicals production to photodegrade CBZ during the photocatalytic process (Duo et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible degradation pathway and mineralization activity
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the photo-degradation products of CBZ were determined through HRMS analysis in negative and positive ion mode., and the possible photo-degradation pathways of MBB and MBC were proposed. For MBB (Scheme S2(a)), there were three probable photo-degradation pathways of CBZ were provided. In pathway Ⅰ, the 10, 11-dihydro-10, 11 epoxy aminophenyl methyl ether (A, m/z = 253) was formed by •O2 − and •OH attacked heterocyclic olefin double bonds of CBZ. Subsequently, An underwent a cleavage reaction of epoxy bond in azepine ring to form B (m/z = 267), which could produce C (m/z = 285) by further aldehyde oxidation (Nawaz et al., 2017). Besides, acridone-N-carbon aldehyde (F, m/z = 224) was formed after hydrogen rearrangement reaction to lose the –CONH2, and detected as hydroxyl adducts of A. Then, F lost aldehyde group and ketone to generate acridine (G, m/z = 180), which could be further oxidized to form H (m/z = 195) (Ahmed et al., 2014). In pathway Ⅱ, the An underwent a hydrolysis process to produce I (m/z = 271). Subsequently, J (m/z = 287) was formed due to the hydrogen rearrangement (Nawaz et al., 2017). In pathway III, hydroxylation of aromatic rings was believed to be essential in the cleavage of sequential rings. The aromatic ring of CBZ was attacked by •OH to form D (m/z = 271), which was further oxidized by h+ to form E (m/z = 152). Finally, all the intermediates could eventually be ring cleavage compounds, CO2, and H2O by •O2 −, •OH, and h+.

As for MBC, the degradation pathway was similar to MBB showed in Scheme S2 (b). There were three probable photo-degradation pathways of CBZ and 8 main intermediates and 2 ring cleavage compounds were detected. In pathway Ⅰ, after light irradiation of LED lamp, the •OH was added to the olefinic double bond on the ring of the CBZ to form hydroxylated CBZ (A, m/z = 253). And then, undergoing a ring condensation reaction, 10-(1-aminovinyl)-10, 10a-dihydroacridine-9carbaldehyde (C, m/z = 253) formed (Begum et al., 2017). D (m/z = 224) was generated by the loss of amides group (-CONH2) and detected as hydroxyl adducts of C. Subsequently, D lost the carboxyl group by oxidation and converted to acridine (H, m/z = 180). In pathway Ⅱ, B could lose the amides group (-CONH2) and generate the G (m/z = 210). In pathway III, the olefinic double-bond of central seven-member ring of CBZ was attacked by •O2 − and •OH and formed 10, 11-dihydro-10, 11 epoxy aminophenyl methyl ether (B, m/z = 253) (Li et al., 2019). As a result, B hydrolyzed into E (m/z = 271) and E was further oxidized to acridone F (m/z = 196). Finally, due to the presence of active species, the 8 intermediates were further converted into 2 main ring-opening compounds I (m/z = 137) and J (m/z = 115), which would be oxidized to CO2 and H2O. The difference between the degradation pathways of MBB and MBC in the degradation of carbamazepine could be due to that under the visible light irradiation, the main reactive oxygen species (ROS) were different. The CQDs could not only transfer electrons but also acted as a receptor and donor of electrons, which could promote the transfer of electrons, making the CB of MIS and the VB of BOC have stronger redox ability, thereby producing more •O2 − and •OH. In this case, because •O2 − and •OH could make the CBZ easier to break bonds and oxidize, more ring-opening products will be generated by MBC photodegraded.

Furthermore, 3D EEM was monitored to investigate the molecular changes of CBZ in the photolysis process (Chen et al., 2017c). As presented in Fig. 6 (a), no obvious fluorescence signal was detected in the original CBZ, which was consistent with the previous literature. However, with the increase of irradiation time, the fluorescence peak at λEx/λEm = (300–400 nm)/(450–550 nm) and λEx/λEm = (200–250 nm)/(400–600 nm) became strong, which were ascribed to the formation of humic acid-like organic matters and fulvic acids (Gao et al., 2017). Both MBB and MBC had this phenomenon during the photocatalytic process, which were due to the decomposition of CBZ into smaller molecules and mineralization of the intermediates in the photodegradation of the aromatic moieties and conjugated heterocycle (Nawaz et al., 2017; Li et al., 2019). Subsequently, TOC was detected to quantify the mineralization degrees of CBZ. As shown in Fig. 6 (b), after 120 min photocatalytic reactions, the TOC removal efficiencies of MBB and MBC could reach 85.09% and 93.79%, respectively, illustrating that almost all CBZ and the intermediates molecules were mineralized into CO2, H2O, and inorganic molecules. Hence, it was reasonable to deduce that CBZ could achieve complete mineralization by extending the reaction time.

ECOSAR program is commonly used to predict the toxicity of the organic compounds and their oxidation intermediates (Carpinteiro et al., 2017). According to the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) of classification and labeling of chemicals, the concentration of acute toxicity and chronic toxicity could be divided into four levels, including not harmful, harmful, toxic, and very toxic as shown in Table S4 (Unece, 2011). Therefore, this work utilized the ECOSAR program to predict the toxicity of CBZ and main intermediates formed by MBB and MBC photodegradation. As displayed in Figure S10, the LC50 values of CBZ towards fish and daphnid were 40.92 and 14.07 mg/L, while the EC50 towards green algae was 0.26 mg/L. And according to the chronic value (Chv) (Figure S11), for CBZ towards fish, daphnid, and green algae were 1.05, 1.17, and 0.1 mg/L. It demonstrated that CBZ was classified as a harmful organic compound to fish and daphnid, and acutely toxic to green algae. As displayed in Figure S10 (a-c), the acute toxicity of products formed by MBB photodegradation system decreased compared with CBZ, but part of them still posed a serious threat to the green algae. For Chv (Figure S11 (a-c)), despite the slightly higher chronic toxicity of product A for fish and product F for daphnid, their concentrations were limited and they would continue to be photodegraded to form simpler non-toxic compounds. Consistent with our prediction, the acute toxicity of products formed by MBC photodegradation was lower than that of MBB (shown in Figure S10 (d-e)), which might be due to the excellent mineralization ability of MBC for CBZ. However, as presented in Figure S11 (d-e), product G with epoxy structure in path Ⅰ exhibited higher chronic toxicity than CBZ towards fish. In addition, the toxicity of the final photodegradation products were not harmful to the aquatic organisms, illustrating that CBZ photodegradation over MBC was of great significance to the safety of the aquatic environment.

In order to evaluate the energy utilization of MBB and MBC for CBZ photodegradation under the LED-lamp irradiation, the electrical energy per order (EEO) was investigated, and a 50 W LED lamp was used as a light source. EEO is calculated through Eq. (2) (Mohammad et al., 2009; Lee et al., 2018):
(2)
E
E o
=
38.4 ∗ P
V ∗ k
where
P
is the LED land power (kW),
V
is the volume of the target pollutant solution,
k
is the pseudo-first-order rate constant. Thence, Table S5 showed the EEO of different photocatalysts for CBZ degradation (Duan et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020b; Tang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019a). Compared with other composites, it was obvious found that MBB and MBC had higher photocatalytic performance and lower energy consumption. And the energy-saving effect of MBC (EEO = 1381) was more obvious than MBB (EEO = 2182). Therefore, the LED lamp as a light source was more economically attractive and more feasible to apply in practice.

The novel and green MgIn2S4/BiOCl/BC (MBB) and MgIn2S4/BiOCl/CQDs (MBC) photocatalysts were successfully fabricated by a two-step method. The different roles of BC and CQDs in forming the MgIn2S4/BiOCl heterojunction interface were discussed in detail. The trapping experiments and EPR measurement indicated that MBC could generate more free radicals during the reaction of photocatalytic degradation of CBZ than MBB, and the main reactive oxygen species for photodegradation of CBZ was superoxide radical (•O2 −). MBC and MBB could harvest natural solar energy for CBZ degradation, the photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of CBZ under natural sunlight were 97.41% and 84.99% and the TOC removal efficiencies were 93.79% and 85.09%, respectively, during 120 min illumination. Meanwhile, the recycling experiments and the characterization of composites after reaction (XRD and FTIR) showed that MBC has higher stability than MBB. In addition, eco-toxicity results from ECOSAR program illustrated that the toxicity of most photodegradation products was much lower than that of CBZ, whether it was in MBB or MBC photocatalytic system. Finally, the evaluation of energy utilization found that MBB and MBC have relatively smaller EEO than other photocatalysts in degradation of CBZ, showed MBB (EEO = 2182) and MBC (EEO = 1381) were more energy-saving than other photocatalysts. In short, the efficient, green, and low-cost MBC and MBB photocatalysts could provide better prospects for removing PPCPs contaminants from water under visible light.
